title: Origins of Christianity
summary: Christianity began as a Jewish movement in 1st‑century Palestine, centered on Jesus of Nazareth and the belief that he was the crucified and risen Messiah. From this small group of Jewish disciples, it grew into a diverse, increasingly Gentile faith that spread across the Roman Empire and developed its own scriptures, institutions, and identity.

concepts.title: Second Temple Judaism
concepts.summary:
Christianity did not appear out of nowhere. It emerged from the religious world of Second Temple Judaism — the form of Judaism that existed from the rebuilding of the Temple in Jerusalem around 515 BCE until its destruction by the Romans in 70 CE. This was the world of Jesus and his earliest followers. It was a Judaism centered on the Temple and its sacrifices, but also increasingly on the Torah, synagogues, and the interpretation of scripture. Within this world, multiple Jewish groups — Pharisees, Sadducees, Essenes, zealots, and others — debated what faithfulness to Israel’s God looked like under foreign rule.

Jews in this period lived under successive empires: Persian, Hellenistic Greek, and finally Roman. Many longed for God to act again in history as dramatically as in the Exodus, to rescue Israel from oppression and restore the kingdom of David. Different groups expressed this hope in different ways, but many shared an expectation of a coming anointed one — a mashiach, or Messiah — who would play a decisive role in God’s plan. When Christianity later claimed that Jesus was this Messiah, it was speaking into this already existing conversation, not inventing the categories from scratch.

sections.title: Context
body: The Second Temple period began when Jewish exiles returned from Babylon under Persian rule and rebuilt the Temple in Jerusalem. For about 500 years, Judaism developed in a small land that was almost always under someone else’s political control — first the Persians, then Alexander the Great and his Hellenistic successors, and finally the Romans. Greek culture and language spread widely after Alexander, creating tensions between traditionalists and those who adopted aspects of Hellenistic life. The Maccabean revolt in the 2nd century BCE, which led to a brief period of Jewish independence, was a reaction against forced Hellenization and temple desecration.

By the time of Jesus (early 1st century CE), Judea had been brought under direct Roman rule. Herod the Great and his sons ruled as client kings under Roman authority, and later Roman prefects such as Pontius Pilate governed Judea directly. Heavy taxation, corrupt local elites, and the visible presence of pagan Roman power in a land devoted to the worship of Israel’s God created constant social and religious tension. Many Jews hoped that God would soon vindicate his people and judge their oppressors.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: Several Jewish groups, or ‘sects,’ shaped the religious environment of the time. The Pharisees emphasized strict observance of the Torah and the ‘oral law’ — a body of interpretive traditions — and believed in resurrection of the dead and the coming judgment. The Sadducees were closely tied to the Temple priesthood and the aristocratic elite; they accepted only the written Torah as authoritative and did not share the Pharisees’ belief in resurrection. The Essenes, a separatist group whose writings are preserved in the Dead Sea Scrolls, withdrew from the Temple system entirely and awaited an imminent, apocalyptic intervention by God.

Zealot movements — not a formal sect so much as a revolutionary current — argued that true faithfulness required active resistance to Roman rule, even violent revolt. At the same time, there were many ordinary Jews who did not belong to any formal group but who expressed their faith through pilgrimage to Jerusalem, attendance at local synagogues, and observance of festivals such as Passover, Shavuot, and Sukkot. Together, these forces created a religious landscape that was both deeply traditional and highly contested, with sharp disagreements about how to live as God’s people under foreign domination.

sections.title: Core idea
body: Second Temple Judaism combined several key elements: devotion to the one God of Israel, loyalty to the Torah as God’s instruction, reverence for the Temple as the place where heaven and earth met, and a hope that God would one day decisively set the world right. The annual cycle of festivals, the daily sacrifices, and the practices of prayer and study all reinforced a sense of covenant identity rooted in scripture and tradition. At the same time, the experience of exile and foreign rule had pushed many Jews to imagine that God’s future salvation would involve not only political change but cosmic transformation.

Apocalyptic literature from this period — such as parts of Daniel and many non-biblical texts — depicts heavenly visions, angelic mediators, judgment scenes, and a coming age in which evil powers would be overthrown. These texts show that ideas about resurrection, final judgment, and a coming ‘Son of Man’ figure were already in circulation before Jesus. Early Christianity did not introduce these themes; it reinterpreted them around the person of Jesus of Nazareth.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: In practical terms, Second Temple Jewish life revolved around three institutions: the Temple, the synagogue, and the home. The Temple in Jerusalem was the only place where certain sacrifices could be offered, so Jews from across the region traveled there for major festivals. Pilgrimage to Jerusalem during Passover — the festival that commemorated the Exodus from Egypt — was particularly significant. It was during one such Passover pilgrimage that Jesus was crucified.

Synagogues, which had developed during and after the Babylonian exile, functioned as local centers for prayer, scripture reading, and teaching. They did not replace the Temple but complemented it. At home, Jews observed practices such as Sabbath rest, dietary laws, and family rituals. Teachers of the law — scribes and Pharisaic scholars — played an important role in interpreting the Torah for everyday life. Jesus’s own activity as a teacher and healer took place squarely within this synagogue-and-village world.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: The religious diversity and tension of Second Temple Judaism created both the problem and the possibility to which Christianity was a response. On the one hand, there was deep discontent with the status quo: many Jews were dissatisfied with corrupt leaders, Roman occupation, and the gap between prophetic promises and present reality. On the other hand, there was a rich vocabulary of hope — Messiah, kingdom of God, resurrection, new covenant — that could be activated to interpret new events.

When Jesus of Nazareth appeared as a charismatic teacher and healer announcing that ‘the kingdom of God is at hand,’ his words resonated precisely because his audience already understood what such language might mean. After his death, when his followers proclaimed that God had raised him and made him Lord and Messiah, they drew on existing Jewish concepts and texts. Without Second Temple Judaism, the message of early Christianity would be unintelligible.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept anchors the entire Origins of Christianity topic in its Jewish setting. The next concept, on Jesus of Nazareth, describes one particular teacher and miracle-worker within this broader world. The later concepts on conflict with Judaism and the emergence of a distinct Christian identity only make sense against the backdrop of the sects, expectations, and institutions of Second Temple Judaism. Understanding this context keeps the story honest: Christianity began as an internal development within Judaism, not as an outside alternative to it.

concepts.title: Jesus of Nazareth
concepts.summary:
Jesus of Nazareth is the central figure of Christianity and a significant figure in world history. For Christians, he is the Messiah and the incarnate Son of God; for historians, he is a Jewish teacher and healer who lived in Roman-occupied Palestine in the early 1st century CE and was executed by crucifixion under the prefect Pontius Pilate. Sorting out what can be said about the ‘historical Jesus’ — the Jesus accessible to historical investigation — from later theological interpretations has been a central task of modern scholarship.

Most historians agree on a basic outline. Jesus was born into a Jewish family, likely in the last years of Herod the Great’s rule, and grew up in Nazareth, a small village in Galilee. He was baptized by John the Baptist, a prophetic figure who called Israel to repentance in anticipation of God’s coming judgment. Jesus then began an independent ministry of teaching, healing, and exorcism, traveling through Galilean towns and villages, proclaiming that the kingdom of God was near. His actions and teaching attracted both enthusiastic crowds and sharp opposition from some religious authorities. Around the year 30 CE, he went to Jerusalem for Passover, clashed with the Temple establishment, and was arrested and crucified by the Romans as a perceived threat to public order.

sections.title: Context
body: Galilee, where Jesus spent most of his life and conducted most of his ministry, was a rural region north of Judea. It was heavily Jewish in population but influenced by Hellenistic culture and economically connected to nearby cities such as Sepphoris and Tiberias. Peasant farmers, day laborers, fishermen, and artisans made up the majority of the population, living close to subsistence level and vulnerable to bad harvests, debt, and taxation. This was the social world in which Jesus preached about God’s care for the poor and warned about the dangers of wealth.

Our primary sources for Jesus’s life are the four canonical Gospels — Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — written several decades after his death by followers who interpreted his life in light of their faith in his resurrection. Non-Christian sources, such as the Jewish historian Josephus and the Roman writer Tacitus, briefly mention Jesus as a real person who was executed under Pilate, providing external confirmation of his historical existence. Scholars read these texts critically, comparing them and situating them in the context of Jewish and Greco-Roman literature, to reconstruct what can be said with reasonable confidence about Jesus’s words and deeds.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: John the Baptist appears at the beginning of all four Gospels as the forerunner of Jesus. He preached a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins in the wilderness by the Jordan River, calling Israel to prepare for God’s imminent judgment. Jesus’s decision to be baptized by John suggests that he initially identified with John’s movement; later, he developed his own distinctive message. The handover from John to Jesus illustrates how new movements could grow out of existing renewal efforts within Judaism.

The disciples — especially the Twelve — were the inner circle of Jesus’s followers. They included figures like Peter, James, and John, who would later lead the Jerusalem church. The Gospels portray them as both devoted and confused: they witness healings and exorcisms, hear parables and teachings, and yet repeatedly misunderstand Jesus’s mission, especially his predictions of suffering and death. Religious authorities such as the Pharisees and chief priests appear as Jesus’s opponents in many Gospel stories, though historically the situation was more complex; conflict within Judaism over interpretation of the law was normal, and not every Pharisee opposed Jesus. Roman authority, embodied in prefects like Pontius Pilate and client kings like Herod Antipas, provided the framework within which Jesus’s fate was ultimately decided.

sections.title: Core idea
body: At the core of Jesus’s message, as presented in the Synoptic Gospels (Matthew, Mark, and Luke), is the proclamation of the ‘kingdom of God’ or ‘kingdom of heaven’ — not a distant place people go after death, but God’s active reign breaking into history. Jesus announces that this kingdom is near, calls people to repent, and embodies the kingdom’s arrival through healings, exorcisms, table fellowship with social outcasts, and teaching that reorients values: the last will be first, the poor are blessed, enemies are to be loved.

Jesus spoke in parables — short, vivid stories that both reveal and conceal his message — and interpreted the Torah in ways that emphasized its underlying intent rather than strict literalism. In the Sermon on the Mount, for example, he intensifies certain commandments (‘You have heard it said… but I say to you…’) to focus on the heart’s orientation, not just outward behavior. His teaching combined ethical rigor, radical mercy, and a sense of urgency about God’s impending action. This combination attracted those who longed for change and threatened those invested in the existing order.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: Jesus’s daily ministry involved itinerant teaching in synagogues and open spaces, healing the sick, casting out unclean spirits, and forming a community around shared meals. He gathered not only the Twelve but also a wider circle of followers, including women who supported the movement materially and were later the first witnesses to the resurrection according to the Gospels. His reputation spread quickly, prompting crowds to seek him out and creating pressure on local authorities to respond.

The critical turning point came when Jesus went up to Jerusalem for Passover. The Gospels describe him entering the city to enthusiastic acclamations and then performing a symbolic act in the Temple — overturning the tables of money changers and driving out merchants, declaring that they had turned a house of prayer into a ‘den of robbers.’ In a volatile festival setting, with Roman troops on edge about potential unrest, this challenge to the Temple establishment was dangerous. Within days, Jesus was arrested, tried before the high priest and the Sanhedrin, handed over to Pilate, and executed by crucifixion — a Roman punishment reserved for rebels and slaves.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: In purely human terms, Jesus’s death looked like the end of a failed movement: another Jewish prophet or would-be Messiah crushed by imperial power. What transformed the story — and gave rise to Christianity — was the conviction among his followers that God had vindicated him by raising him from the dead. That conviction, explored in the next concept, reinterpreted his shameful death as a redemptive act and his life as the decisive revelation of God’s character.

Even apart from later theological developments, the historical Jesus left a deep imprint. His teaching on love of enemies, care for the poor, and the inversion of social hierarchies has influenced religious and secular ethics for centuries. Modern scholarship’s insistence that Jesus be understood as a Jew of his time has also helped correct earlier Christian tendencies to oppose ‘Jesus’ to ‘Judaism’ — a distortion that fueled centuries of anti-Jewish sentiment. Recovering Jesus within his Jewish context is essential both historically and theologically.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept sits at the center of the topic: every other concept either prepares for Jesus (Second Temple Judaism), flows from him (the Jerusalem community, Paul’s mission), or interprets his significance (the development of doctrine and scripture). The next concept, on crucifixion and resurrection faith, picks up at the moment of Jesus’s death and follows his followers as they grapple with what it meant — an interpretive process that turned a Roman execution into the cornerstone of a global religion.

concepts.title: Crucifixion & Resurrection Faith
concepts.summary:
The execution of Jesus by crucifixion around 30 CE is one of the best-attested facts about his life. Crucifixion was a brutal Roman punishment reserved for rebels, slaves, and the lowest criminals, designed not only to kill but to humiliate and terrorize. From a historical standpoint, Jesus likely died because Roman authorities, prompted by local leaders, judged him a political threat during a tense Passover season. Yet within weeks of his death, his followers were publicly proclaiming in Jerusalem that God had raised him from the dead and made him ‘both Lord and Messiah.’ This conviction — that the crucified Jesus had been vindicated and exalted by God — is the heart of earliest Christianity.

The New Testament does not describe the resurrection as something anyone saw directly happening; what it reports are experiences of the empty tomb and of encounters with the risen Jesus. Different Gospels present these experiences with different details, but all agree that women were the first discoverers of the empty tomb and that the disciples, who had fled in fear, were transformed into bold witnesses. Paul’s letters, written 20–30 years after Jesus’s death, preserve even earlier creeds that speak of Jesus dying for sins, being buried, and being raised on the third day. For the earliest Christians, the resurrection was not a vague symbol of hope but a specific, historical act of God that signaled the beginning of the general resurrection and the dawn of a new age.

sections.title: Context
body: Crucifixion was a public spectacle. Victims were often scourged, forced to carry their crossbeam to the execution site, stripped, and nailed or tied to the cross, where they might hang for hours or days before dying of asphyxiation, blood loss, or shock. The message to the population was clear: this is what happens to those who challenge Rome. The charge nailed above Jesus’s head — ‘King of the Jews’ — indicates that Rome saw him, or at least chose to present him, as a royal pretender and thus a political threat, even if his actual aims were more spiritual than revolutionary.

Jewish expectations about resurrection at the time were varied. Many Pharisees believed in a future bodily resurrection of the righteous at the end of days; Sadducees, by contrast, denied resurrection altogether. No one expected an individual to be raised in the middle of history while the rest of the dead remained in their graves. This is what makes the earliest Christian claim distinctive: they did not say that Jesus’s ‘soul went to heaven’ — a common idea in the Greco-Roman world — but that God had begun the final resurrection with him as the ‘firstfruits.’

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: Peter (also called Simon Peter) emerges in the Acts of the Apostles as the leading spokesman for the resurrection faith. In his speeches in Jerusalem, he interprets Jesus’s death and resurrection through the lens of Israel’s scriptures, citing Psalms and prophets to argue that God had always planned to raise his ‘holy one’ and make him both Lord and Messiah. Peter’s own story — from denying Jesus three times to boldly preaching in the Temple courts — is presented as evidence of the transformative power of the resurrection.

Paul of Tarsus, a former persecutor of the Jesus movement, became its most influential theologian and missionary after what he described as an encounter with the risen Christ on the road to Damascus. In 1 Corinthians 15, written around 54 CE, Paul quotes an earlier formulaic tradition: that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures, was buried, was raised on the third day, and appeared to Cephas (Peter), the Twelve, more than five hundred believers at once, James, all the apostles, and finally to Paul himself. This is one of our earliest written witnesses to the resurrection faith, predating the Gospels.

sections.title: Core idea
body: Theologically, the crucifixion and resurrection were interpreted by early Christians as a single saving event. Jesus’s death was not seen as a tragic accident but as a sacrifice ‘for sins’ — an act that somehow dealt with the power of sin and reconciled humanity to God. Different New Testament writings use different metaphors: sacrifice, ransom, victory over hostile powers, legal justification. The resurrection was God’s public vindication of Jesus’s life and teaching, a declaration that Rome’s judgment had been overturned in the highest court.

The earliest Christian confession, ‘Jesus is Lord,’ encapsulated this conviction. In the Roman world, ‘Lord’ (Kyrios) was a title associated with the emperor and with the God of Israel in Greek translations of the Bible. To say ‘Jesus is Lord’ was therefore both a religious and a political claim: allegiance to Jesus took precedence over allegiance to Caesar. The resurrection turned a crucified Galilean into the focal point of ultimate loyalty for communities scattered across the empire.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: In practice, resurrection faith expressed itself through proclamation, baptism, and communal life. The earliest disciples in Jerusalem began publicly announcing that God had raised Jesus and calling their fellow Jews to repent and be baptized in his name. Baptism — an immersion in water — functioned as a visible entry into the new community and a symbolic participation in Jesus’s death and resurrection: going down into the water as dying with Christ, emerging as rising with him to new life.

Resurrection faith also reshaped how these communities understood scripture. Texts that had long been read in other ways — such as Isaiah’s ‘suffering servant’ passages or certain Psalms — were now interpreted as prophetic foreshadowings of Jesus’s death and vindication. Early Christian preaching is full of this rereading of Israel’s scriptures. The weekly gathering on ‘the first day of the week’ — Sunday — instead of the traditional Sabbath reflected the centrality of the resurrection morning in their identity and worship.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: The belief that God had raised Jesus from the dead turned a demoralized, frightened group of disciples into the nucleus of a movement willing to face persecution and death rather than deny their witness. Historically, this transformation is one of the key data points that scholars, even those who do not share the faith claim, seek to explain. Whatever one concludes about what really happened, the fact that early Christians were convinced enough to stake their lives on it is undeniable.

Over time, the crucifixion and resurrection became the central symbols of Christian identity and theology. They shaped Christian art (the cross), liturgy (Good Friday and Easter), sacraments (baptism and Eucharist), and ethics (a call to self-giving love modeled on Jesus’s own self-offering). The interpretation of these events would generate centuries of theological debate, but the basic pattern — death, then vindication; humiliation, then exaltation — became the core narrative by which Christians understand both Jesus’s story and their own.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept bridges the life of Jesus and the life of the early church. The Jerusalem community described in the next concept is, in a real sense, a community organized around the memory of the crucified Jesus and the conviction of his living presence. Paul’s later letters, the development of Christian worship, and the eventual creeds of the church all build on this foundational claim. Without the resurrection faith, there would be no Christianity; Jesus would likely be remembered, if at all, as one more obscure Galilean teacher executed by Rome.

concepts.title: The Jerusalem Church
concepts.summary:
After Jesus’s death, the first community of his followers formed in Jerusalem. This group, often called the Jerusalem church, was made up almost entirely of Jews who continued to worship at the Temple, keep the Torah, and see themselves as part of Israel — not as members of a new religion. What distinguished them was their conviction that God had raised Jesus from the dead and exalted him as Lord and Messiah. They believed that in Jesus, God had begun to fulfill the ancient promises to Israel and that the end of the age was near.

The Acts of the Apostles portrays this community as living a shared life of prayer, teaching, and mutual care. They met in the Temple courts and in homes, shared possessions so that no one was in need, and devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching about Jesus. Leadership gradually crystallized around figures such as Peter and James, the brother of Jesus. From this small, Torah‑observant, Jerusalem‑based group, the message about Jesus would spread outward to Judea, Samaria, and eventually the wider Roman world.

sections.title: Context
body: The Jerusalem church emerged in the turbulent years following Jesus’s crucifixion, likely in the early 30s CE. Jerusalem at this time was the religious and cultural heart of Judaism, dominated by the Temple and the festivals that drew pilgrims from across the diaspora. Roman prefects, including Pontius Pilate, maintained order with the help of local elites and the Temple priesthood. Messianic hopes and apocalyptic expectations were widespread, and tensions between the population and Roman authority periodically erupted into violence.

Within this charged environment, the proclamation that a recently crucified Galilean had been raised from the dead and made ‘Lord’ was both exciting and risky. The early followers of Jesus navigated a delicate balance: they continued to participate in Temple worship and saw themselves as part of Israel’s story, yet they claimed that God had done something radically new in Jesus. Acts describes them as enjoying ‘the goodwill of all the people’ at first, but also facing opposition from some authorities as their message spread.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: Peter is the central figure in the early chapters of Acts. He preaches the first public sermons about Jesus in Jerusalem, interprets the resurrection as the fulfillment of Israel’s scriptures, and performs healings in Jesus’s name. His boldness after previously denying Jesus is presented as a sign of the Spirit’s power. James, identified as the brother of Jesus, emerges later as the key leader of the Jerusalem community — respected for his piety and sometimes called ‘James the Just’ in early Christian tradition.

The apostles as a group — the Twelve — served as eyewitnesses to Jesus’s ministry and resurrection, giving the movement continuity and authority. Opposition came primarily from some members of the Temple leadership, who saw the apostles’ preaching as a threat to their authority and to public order. The persecution under Herod Agrippa I, including the execution of James the son of Zebedee and the imprisonment of Peter, shows that the movement quickly attracted serious pushback. Yet the Jerusalem church persisted, adapting its leadership and practices as circumstances changed.

sections.title: Core idea
body: The Jerusalem community understood itself not as a new religion, but as the restored and renewed people of God within Israel. They believed that in Jesus, God had acted decisively to inaugurate the ‘last days’ — the time when the Spirit would be poured out on all flesh, as the prophet Joel had foretold. Their common life, marked by shared possessions, care for the poor, and constant prayer, was meant to embody the values of the kingdom of God that Jesus had proclaimed.

Central to their identity was the experience of the Holy Spirit, especially as narrated in the Pentecost story in Acts 2. There, the disciples are depicted as being filled with the Spirit, speaking in other languages, and proclaiming God’s deeds of power to pilgrims from many nations. This event was interpreted as the fulfillment of God’s promise to dwell with his people in a new way, not in a physical Temple building but in a community of believers. The church, in this sense, became a living temple.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: The daily life of the Jerusalem church combined Temple worship with gatherings in homes. Acts describes believers devoting themselves to the apostles’ teaching, to fellowship, to the breaking of bread, and to prayers. ‘Breaking of bread’ likely refers both to ordinary meals and to a special ritual meal in memory of Jesus — the precursor of what later became the Eucharist or Lord’s Supper. Economic sharing was radical: those who owned property sold it and laid the proceeds at the apostles’ feet to be distributed according to need.

The community also engaged in active mission. Peter and John healed a man at the Temple gate, leading to public preaching and the conversion of thousands according to Acts’s narrative. As persecution increased, some believers were scattered to surrounding regions, unintentionally spreading the message. The martyrdom of Stephen, one of the first deacons, marks a turning point: it intensifies persecution in Jerusalem but also propels the movement outward, setting the stage for the wider Gentile mission.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: The Jerusalem church established patterns that would shape Christian life for centuries: communal worship, shared meals, care for the poor, and leadership by recognized teachers and elders. It also set a precedent for understanding the church as both continuous with Israel’s story and distinct in its confession of Jesus as Lord. The decisions and practices of this early community became a touchstone for later debates about doctrine and discipline.

Historically, the Jerusalem church remained a significant center of the Jesus movement until the Jewish‑Roman war of 66–73 CE, which culminated in the destruction of the Temple. According to later tradition, many Jewish Christians fled Jerusalem before the final siege. After 70 CE, the demographic and theological center of Christianity shifted increasingly toward predominantly Gentile communities in cities like Antioch, Ephesus, and Rome. Yet the memory of the Jerusalem community continued to function as an idealized model of unity and faithfulness.

sections.title: Connections
body: The Jerusalem church is the bridge between the resurrection faith and the wider expansion of Christianity. The next concept, on Paul and the Gentile mission, traces how the movement moved beyond Jerusalem to non‑Jewish audiences. The later concept on conflict with Judaism will revisit Jerusalem at the Council described in Acts 15, where leaders grappled with whether Gentile believers needed to keep the Jewish law — a debate that would redefine the relationship between Christianity and its Jewish roots.

concepts.title: Paul & the Gentile Mission
concepts.summary:
If the Jerusalem church represents the first, Jewish phase of the Jesus movement, Paul of Tarsus represents its explosive expansion into the Gentile world. Born a Jew in the diaspora and trained as a Pharisee, Paul initially opposed the movement, seeing it as a dangerous deviation from true Judaism. According to his own letters and the Acts of the Apostles, he participated in the persecution of believers before experiencing a dramatic encounter with the risen Christ on the road to Damascus. This experience transformed him from persecutor to apostle — a ‘sent one’ — to the nations.

Over the next decades, Paul traveled throughout the eastern Mediterranean, founding and nurturing communities of Jesus‑followers in cities like Antioch, Corinth, Philippi, and Thessalonica. His core message was that in Jesus the crucified and risen Messiah, God had opened a way for both Jews and Gentiles to be reconciled to God by faith, not by works of the law. Gentiles, he argued, did not need to become Jews — through circumcision and full Torah observance — to be included in God’s people. Instead, they were justified by faith in Christ and received the Holy Spirit as a sign of their inclusion. This stance made Paul both the architect of a genuinely trans‑ethnic Christianity and a controversial figure in his own time.

sections.title: Context
body: The Roman Empire of the first century was a patchwork of cities connected by well‑built roads and sea routes, facilitating travel and communication on a scale not seen before in the Mediterranean. Greek served as a common language across much of the empire, and urban centers were religiously pluralistic, with temples, synagogues, and philosophical schools existing side by side. Diaspora Jewish communities were present in many cities, providing natural starting points for Paul’s work: he could begin in the synagogue, speaking to fellow Jews and ‘God‑fearers’ (Gentiles attracted to Judaism), and then widen his outreach.

Within the Jesus movement, the inclusion of Gentiles posed a pressing theological and practical question. Jesus and the original apostles were Jews; the early community in Jerusalem observed the Torah. As Gentiles began to respond to the message, believers had to decide whether full conversion to Judaism was required. Some Jewish Christians insisted on circumcision and law observance for Gentile converts. Paul’s mission, and the debates it provoked, unfolded against this background of inter‑Christian disagreement and Jewish‑Gentile tension in the wider society.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: Paul himself is the key figure in this concept. In his letters — our earliest Christian writings — he describes himself as a Hebrew of Hebrews and a Pharisee, zealous for the traditions of his ancestors. His encounter with Christ, which he characterizes as a revelation of God’s Son ‘in’ him, convinced him that God had acted decisively in Jesus to include the nations in the covenant. From that point, his life was oriented around preaching this good news, often at great personal cost: he recounts beatings, imprisonments, shipwrecks, and constant opposition.

Other important figures include Barnabas, who initially vouched for Paul before the wary Jerusalem community and partnered with him on his first missionary journey; Silas and Timothy, who accompanied him on later journeys; and leaders in Jerusalem such as Peter and James, whose stance toward Paul’s Gentile mission is a central concern of Paul’s letter to the Galatians and the narrative of Acts 15. Roman authorities, city magistrates, and local opponents — both Jewish and Gentile — also play a role, sometimes welcoming Paul, sometimes expelling or imprisoning him.

sections.title: Core idea
body: Paul’s central theological claim was that a person is justified — put into right relationship with God — by faith in Jesus Christ, not by ‘works of the law.’ For Paul, the law (Torah) was holy and good, but it could not ultimately solve the problem of human sin; it revealed the problem more clearly. In Christ’s death and resurrection, Paul believed, God had dealt with sin and opened a new way of belonging to God’s people that did not depend on ethnic origin or law observance. Jew and Gentile alike were to be united ‘in Christ’ as one new humanity.

This did not mean that Paul rejected his Jewish identity; he continued to see Israel’s scriptures as authoritative and understood Jesus as the fulfillment of God’s promises to Abraham. But it did mean that he opposed making circumcision or dietary laws a requirement for Gentile believers. In letters like Galatians and Romans, he argues passionately that to impose the law on Gentiles would be to misunderstand the gospel and to rebuild the very barriers that Christ had torn down.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: Practically, Paul’s mission involved traveling from city to city, often staying for months or years to establish and strengthen communities. He supported himself at times through manual labor — working as a tentmaker — and at other times received support from the churches he had founded. He preached in synagogues, public squares, and private homes, adapting his approach to different audiences: quoting scripture and engaging in detailed exegesis with Jews, and appealing to philosophical ideas and local religious practices when addressing Gentiles, as in his famous speech at the Areopagus in Athens.

Paul’s letters functioned as ongoing pastoral and theological guidance to these communities. He addressed conflicts, moral failures, questions about marriage, food, spiritual gifts, and the return of Christ. These letters were read aloud in worship gatherings and circulated between churches, gradually acquiring authority as written expressions of apostolic teaching. The very practice of writing such letters helped knit scattered communities into a network that shared a common identity and core beliefs despite cultural and geographic differences.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: Paul’s insistence that Gentiles could belong to God’s people without becoming Jews was a turning point in the history of Christianity. It enabled the movement to become genuinely global, not limited to one ethnic group or bound to one land. Over time, as Gentile believers came to outnumber Jewish believers, Paul’s vision of a multiethnic body ‘in Christ’ became the dominant reality of the church — though often without the continued Jewish participation that Paul himself had envisioned and hoped for.

At the same time, Paul’s complex relationship with the law and with Israel has been a source of both inspiration and controversy. His writings have been interpreted in ways that contributed to Christian supersessionism — the idea that the church replaced Israel — and to negative attitudes toward Judaism. Modern scholarship and Jewish‑Christian dialogue have sought to reread Paul in a way that respects his ongoing loyalty to his people and sees his work as an intra‑Jewish debate about how God was fulfilling his promises, rather than as a rejection of Judaism itself.

sections.title: Connections
body: Paul’s mission connects the Jerusalem church to the wider Roman world. The conflict over Gentile inclusion, which comes to a head in the ‘Council of Jerusalem’ described in Acts 15, is explored more fully in the next concept on tensions with Judaism. Paul’s letters are also a crucial source for understanding the later concepts in this topic: they are foundational texts for the development of Christian doctrine, for the formation of the New Testament canon, and for the emerging Christian identity that would eventually distinguish itself from Judaism.

concepts.title: Tensions with Judaism
concepts.summary:
Christianity began as a movement within Judaism, but within a few generations it had become a largely Gentile religion increasingly distinct from its Jewish roots. This shift did not happen overnight or through a single break; it unfolded through a series of tensions and conflicts over questions such as: Is Jesus the Messiah? Must Gentile believers keep the Jewish law? How should followers of Jesus relate to the Temple, the festivals, and the wider Jewish community? The answers different groups gave to these questions gradually pulled them apart.

The New Testament itself bears witness to these debates. Paul’s letters chronicle fierce arguments over circumcision and Torah observance for Gentiles. The Acts of the Apostles recounts the Council of Jerusalem, where leaders decided that Gentiles did not need to be circumcised but should abstain from certain practices offensive to Jewish believers. The Gospels, written after significant conflict had already arisen, sometimes portray disputes between Jesus and Jewish leaders in ways colored by later tensions. After the destruction of the Temple in 70 CE and especially after the failed Bar Kokhba revolt (132–135 CE), boundaries hardened: rabbinic Judaism and what we now call Christianity developed along increasingly separate paths, each defining itself partly in contrast to the other.

sections.title: Context
body: In the earliest years, followers of Jesus were simply one more Jewish group among many. They worshipped the God of Israel, revered the Torah and the prophets, and participated in Jewish communal life. Their distinctive claim — that Jesus was the risen Messiah — initially sparked controversy but did not immediately place them outside the bounds of Judaism. The Temple still stood, and Jewish Christians in Jerusalem continued to worship there alongside other Jews.

As the movement spread into the Gentile world, however, the social and religious realities changed. In mixed communities of Jewish and Gentile believers, questions arose about table fellowship, purity laws, and festival observance. At the same time, many Jews who did not accept the Christian claims about Jesus saw the new movement as a threat or a misunderstanding of Israel’s hope. Local conflicts, both intra‑Jewish and between Christians and civic authorities, contributed to a gradual perception of difference on both sides.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: The Council of Jerusalem, described in Acts 15, brought together key leaders — including Peter, James, and Paul — to address the question of Gentile converts. Some believers from a Pharisaic background insisted that Gentiles must be circumcised and required to keep the law of Moses. After much debate, the council concluded that Gentiles should not be burdened with the full yoke of the law but should abstain from idolatry, sexual immorality, and eating blood or meat from strangled animals. This compromise acknowledged Jewish concerns while affirming Paul’s mission.

Beyond internal debates, broader historical forces drove the wedge between Judaism and Christianity. The Jewish‑Roman wars (66–73 CE and 132–135 CE) devastated Judea and reshaped Jewish life. After 70 CE, with the Temple destroyed, Pharisaic‑rabbinic Judaism reorganized around Torah study and synagogue life. Some scholars argue that Jewish Christians, who accepted Gentiles and reinterpreted Israel’s scriptures around Jesus, were increasingly seen as deviating from the emerging rabbinic consensus. On the Christian side, the growth of predominantly Gentile churches, especially in cities like Rome, meant that many believers had little direct connection to Judaism and sometimes defined their faith in contrast to ‘the Jews.’

sections.title: Core idea
body: At the heart of the tension was the question of identity: who are the people of God, and on what basis? Jewish tradition grounded identity in God’s covenant with Israel, marked by circumcision, Torah observance, and participation in the life of the Jewish community. The earliest Christians claimed that Jesus had brought this covenant to its fulfillment and that faith in him was now the decisive marker. For many Jewish believers, this faith was an intensification of their Jewish identity; for many non‑believing Jews, it looked like a distortion.

As Christian communities became more Gentile, some adopted the view that the church had replaced Israel as God’s people — an idea later called supersessionism. This view contrasted ‘grace’ with ‘law’ and sometimes caricatured Judaism as a religion of legalism and works. Such contrasts oversimplified both traditions and ignored the deep continuity between them. Recognizing how these theological battles were shaped by historical conflict helps prevent repeating their mistakes.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: Practically, the separation occurred through a thousand local decisions and conflicts rather than a single decree. In some cities, Jewish synagogues expelled followers of Jesus; in others, Jewish and Gentile believers gradually formed separate meeting places. Christian communities shifted their primary day of worship to Sunday, the day of Jesus’s resurrection, while many Jewish Christians continued to observe the Sabbath as well. Over time, some Christian leaders discouraged observance of Jewish festivals and practices among Gentile believers to avoid confusion and conflict.

On the Jewish side, emerging rabbinic authorities worked to define the boundaries of the community after the Temple’s destruction. Some scholars point to the Birkat ha‑Minim, a prayer that may have been formulated to exclude certain ‘heretics,’ possibly including Jewish Christians, from synagogue life. Whether or not this prayer specifically targeted Christians, it reflects the broader process of boundary‑drawing that made it increasingly difficult to belong fully to both communities. By the end of the 2nd century, most Christians and Jews saw themselves as members of distinct religions, even if they shared scriptures and many practices.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: The gradual separation of Christianity from Judaism had profound and often tragic consequences. As Christianity gained political power in the Roman Empire, especially after Constantine in the 4th century, some Christians used theological arguments about supersession and Jewish ‘unbelief’ to justify discriminatory laws, forced conversions, and violence against Jewish communities. Anti‑Judaism, rooted in misreadings of New Testament polemics and a failure to remember Christianity’s Jewish origins, became a persistent stain on Christian history.

At the same time, the separation allowed both traditions to develop distinctive strengths. Rabbinic Judaism created a rich culture of law, study, and communal practice that sustained Jewish life through centuries of diaspora. Christianity developed global missionary institutions, creeds, and theological systems centered on Jesus. In recent decades, renewed Jewish‑Christian dialogue has sought to heal the wounds of the past, emphasizing that Christianity cannot be understood apart from Judaism and that respect for the ongoing Jewish covenant is essential for Christian self‑understanding.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept explains how a movement that began as a Jewish sect came to be perceived — by both insiders and outsiders — as a separate religion. It is closely linked to the previous concept on Paul and the Gentile mission, since debates about Gentile inclusion were a primary driver of tension. It also prepares the ground for later concepts in this topic on the Roman context, the development of Christian scripture, and the emergence of a distinct Christian identity in the first few centuries, all of which assume a widening gap between church and synagogue.

concepts.title: The Roman World
concepts.summary:
Christianity spread and took shape within the vast, complex world of the Roman Empire. This empire stretched from Britain to Egypt and from Spain to Syria, uniting diverse peoples under a single political system. Its roads, shipping routes, and common languages (especially Greek) made long‑distance travel and communication far easier than in earlier eras. At the same time, Roman rule was maintained by military force, heavy taxation, and a demand for loyalty to the emperor. Local religions, including Judaism, were usually tolerated as long as they did not threaten public order or imperial authority.

This environment created both opportunities and dangers for the early Christian movement. On the one hand, Christian missionaries like Paul could travel relatively safely between cities, write letters that would be carried along trade routes, and find audiences in cosmopolitan urban centers. On the other hand, Christians’ refusal to participate in certain civic religious practices — such as offering sacrifice to the emperor’s genius — could be perceived as disloyalty. Understanding the Roman world helps explain how Christianity could grow from a tiny Jewish sect to a significant presence across the empire within a few centuries, and why it sometimes faced suspicion and persecution along the way.

sections.title: Context
body: The Roman Empire of the first three centuries CE was politically centralized but culturally diverse. Local cities retained their own traditions, languages, and gods, while also participating in a shared imperial culture that celebrated Rome’s power and the emperor’s status. Public life was saturated with religion: festivals, sacrifices, and civic rituals honored both traditional deities and the imperial family. Refusing to participate in these acts could be seen as antisocial or subversive, since religion and politics were deeply intertwined.

Judaism occupied a unique position within this world. As an ancient, ethnically based religion with a strong sense of identity and a strict monotheism that rejected idols, it was granted certain exemptions — for example, Jews were typically allowed to offer prayers for the emperor instead of sacrifices to him. Jewish communities were found in many cities, particularly around the eastern Mediterranean. Early Christianity, emerging from Judaism and sharing its monotheism, inherited both some of these protections and some of the suspicions directed toward Jews.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: Roman emperors such as Augustus, Tiberius, and Claudius provided the political framework within which early Christianity developed, even if they had little direct interaction with it at first. Later emperors, notably Nero and Domitian, are associated in Christian memory with localized persecutions, though the historical evidence suggests that empire‑wide, systematic persecutions were rare before the 3rd century. Provincial governors, city magistrates, and local elites often had more immediate impact on Christian communities than the distant emperor.

Two structural forces were particularly important: the Roman road network and the system of urbanization. Well‑built roads and relatively safe sea lanes enabled missionaries and letters to move quickly between key cities. Urban centers like Antioch, Corinth, Ephesus, and Rome became hubs where ideas, goods, and people from across the empire mixed. Synagogues in these cities provided initial contact points for Christian preaching, while broader urban populations offered a wider field for mission. The combination of mobility and urban concentration meant that a message could spread rapidly once it gained traction.

sections.title: Core idea
body: Theologically, early Christians claimed that Jesus was ‘Lord’ in a world where ‘Lord’ was also a title of the emperor. This did not necessarily mean that Christians were plotting political revolution, but it did mean that their ultimate allegiance belonged to someone other than Caesar. Their refusal to offer sacrifices to the emperor or to participate in rituals they saw as idolatrous set them apart in a culture where civic and religious duties were intertwined. To their neighbors and officials, this could look like stubbornness or even atheism (in the ancient sense of rejecting the gods).

At the same time, Christian communities adopted certain aspects of Roman organizational culture. They developed forms of leadership (bishops, presbyters, deacons), networks of communication, and charitable structures that functioned effectively within urban settings. The Christian concept of the ekklesia — an assembly called together — resonated with existing civic assemblies while reorienting the idea around worship of Christ and mutual support. Christianity was both countercultural and deeply embedded in its Roman context.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: Practically, the Roman context shaped how Christian mission operated. Missionaries traveled along established trade routes, often targeting major cities where they could reach diverse populations and from which the message could radiate outward into surrounding regions. House churches — gatherings of believers in private homes — became the primary setting for worship, teaching, and mutual support. These small, flexible communities could function without dedicated buildings and could adapt quickly to local conditions.

When conflicts arose with authorities, the Roman legal system provided both threats and protections. In some cases, Christians were brought before magistrates and pressured to renounce their faith and sacrifice to the gods; refusal could lead to punishment or execution. In other cases, officials treated Christianity as a minor superstition and sought to avoid unrest by handling cases pragmatically. The famous correspondence between the governor Pliny the Younger and Emperor Trajan around 112 CE shows a Roman official trying to figure out how to deal with Christians: he is more concerned with their stubborn refusal to conform than with any specific crime.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: The Roman imperial framework made the rapid spread of Christianity possible. Without a common language, road system, and relatively stable political order, it would have been far more difficult for a small movement originating in a provincial corner of the empire to reach cities thousands of miles away within a few generations. Christianity’s eventual rise to prominence — culminating in its legalization under Constantine and its gradual adoption as the empire’s favored religion — cannot be separated from this infrastructure.

At the same time, the entanglement with Roman power brought lasting ambiguities. Once Christianity began to enjoy imperial favor in the 4th century, it had to navigate its relationship to the state differently, sometimes becoming the persecutor rather than the persecuted. The early period, when Christianity was a marginal, sometimes suspect movement under Roman rule, remained an important reference point for later Christians wrestling with questions of power, persecution, and faithfulness.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept situates the entire Origins of Christianity story within its broader political and cultural environment. Paul’s mission, the spread of house churches, and the eventual formation of a trans‑local Christian network all depended on Roman roads, cities, and laws. The later developments in Christian scripture and identity — covered in the next two concepts — also unfolded within this Roman matrix, as debates about which texts to read and what to believe were shaped by councils, controversies, and communication across the empire.

concepts.title: From Oral Tradition to New Testament
concepts.summary:
The first followers of Jesus did not immediately sit down to write a new Bible. For decades, the Jesus movement lived by oral tradition and by reading Israel’s scriptures — what Christians later called the Old Testament — in a new light. Stories about Jesus’s words and deeds were told and retold in sermons, in teaching sessions, and around shared meals. Letters were written to address specific communities’ questions and struggles. Only gradually were these oral and written materials collected, edited, and recognized as authoritative scriptures alongside the Jewish Bible. The result of this process is what Christians now call the New Testament.

The New Testament consists of 27 books: four Gospels, the Acts of the Apostles, 21 letters (epistles), and the book of Revelation. These texts were written between roughly 50 and 100 CE by various authors in Greek. They reflect different perspectives and emphases but share a common focus on Jesus’s life, death, and resurrection and their implications for the people of God. The process by which certain writings were included in the canon and others were not took several centuries and was guided by criteria such as apostolic origin, widespread use in churches, and consistency with the core proclamation about Christ.

sections.title: Context
body: In the earliest period, the authoritative scriptures for Jesus’s followers were the books Jews already revered: the Law, the Prophets, and the Writings — the Tanakh. These texts were read in synagogues and interpreted in light of the conviction that Jesus was the promised Messiah. Passages from Isaiah, the Psalms, and other books were re‑read as foreshadowing his suffering, death, and vindication. At the same time, the Jesus movement generated new material: sayings, parables, miracle stories, and accounts of the passion (Jesus’s arrest, trial, and crucifixion) circulated orally in Aramaic and Greek.

Paul’s letters, written between about 50 and 60 CE, are the earliest Christian writings we possess. They were occasional documents — written to specific communities to address particular issues — but were quickly copied, shared, and read in other churches. By the end of the first century, collections of Paul’s letters were circulating. The Gospels were written somewhat later: Mark probably around 70 CE, Matthew and Luke in the 80s–90s, and John near the end of the century. Each Gospel drew on oral traditions and earlier written sources to present a theologically shaped portrait of Jesus for a particular audience.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: The evangelists — the authors of the four canonical Gospels — were key shapers of Christian memory. By selecting, arranging, and interpreting stories about Jesus, they preserved both shared traditions and distinctive emphases. Mark’s fast‑paced narrative presents Jesus as the suffering Son of God whose identity is fully revealed at the cross. Matthew emphasizes Jesus as the fulfillment of Jewish scripture and as a teacher like a new Moses. Luke highlights God’s concern for the poor and marginalized and the universal scope of salvation. John offers a more overtly theological portrait, focusing on Jesus’s divine identity and his intimate relationship with the Father.

Church leaders and communities played a crucial role in the process of canon formation. As various gospels, letters, and apocalyptic writings circulated, churches had to discern which texts to read publicly in worship and to use as authoritative guides. Criteria emerged: apostolic connection (written by an apostle or a close associate), widespread use across diverse churches, and consistency with the ‘rule of faith’ — the basic summary of Christian belief. Debates arose over certain books (such as Revelation, Hebrews, and some of the shorter letters), and some writings used by particular groups (like the Gospel of Thomas or the Shepherd of Hermas) were eventually not included in the canon.

sections.title: Core idea
body: The shift from oral tradition to written scripture did not replace living memory and teaching; it stabilized and guided them. Writing down the Gospels and letters allowed communities separated by distance and time to share a common narrative and set of teachings about Jesus. The New Testament canon, once formed, functioned as a norm — a measuring stick — for Christian belief and practice. It did not end debate, but it provided a shared set of texts within which debate could take place.

For early Christians, the New Testament did not stand alone but in relationship with the Jewish scriptures. The Old Testament and New Testament together told a single overarching story: creation, covenant, Israel’s history, the coming of Christ, the formation of the church, and the hope for future renewal. Reading the two testaments together — sometimes harmoniously, sometimes in tension — became a central task of Christian theology. How to relate the covenants, how to understand the law in light of Christ, and how to interpret prophetic fulfillment remained live questions for centuries.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: Practically, the formation of the New Testament canon unfolded through usage and recognition rather than through a single official decree. Churches read certain texts regularly in worship, copied them, and shared them with others. Over time, a core collection — the four Gospels, Acts, the Pauline letters, and a few others — gained nearly universal acceptance. Church fathers such as Irenaeus in the 2nd century argued explicitly for the authority of four and only four Gospels, rejecting both attempts to reduce them to a single harmony and proposals to add new ones.

Regional councils in the 4th century, such as those at Hippo (393) and Carthage (397), issued lists of New Testament books that match the 27‑book canon used by most Christians today. These councils did not create the canon so much as ratify existing practice. The process was gradual, contested at the margins, and intertwined with broader theological controversies, such as debates over the nature of Christ and the Trinity. Once established, the New Testament shaped Christian liturgy, preaching, doctrine, and personal devotion in enduring ways.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: The emergence of the New Testament alongside the Old Testament gave Christianity a two‑part scripture that has profoundly influenced global culture. The stories of Jesus, the parables, the Sermon on the Mount, the letters of Paul, and the apocalyptic visions of Revelation have inspired art, music, law, ethics, and politics for nearly two millennia. At the same time, differing interpretations of these texts have led to sharp disagreements and the formation of multiple denominations and traditions.

For the relationship between Christianity and Judaism, the New Testament canon was a double‑edged development. On one hand, it preserved the movement’s Jewish roots by enshrining the Old Testament as scripture. On the other, some New Testament passages, read without context, have been used to justify negative views of Jews and Judaism. Modern scholarship and many church statements have emphasized that the New Testament must be read with awareness of its intra‑Jewish debates and with respect for the ongoing validity of the Jewish covenant.

sections.title: Connections
body: This concept ties directly to Paul’s letters, the Jerusalem community’s preaching, and the broader Roman context in which texts circulated. It sets up the final concept on the emergence of a distinct Christian identity by showing how shared scriptures and authoritative writings helped define what counted as genuinely ‘Christian.’ The formation of the New Testament canon also mirrors the process by which rabbinic Judaism recognized its own core texts, highlighting again the intertwined yet diverging paths of the two traditions.

concepts.title: From Sect to Religion
concepts.summary:
By the end of the first century, the Jesus movement had spread throughout the eastern Roman Empire and was present as far west as Rome itself. Over the next two centuries, it continued to grow, diversify, and define itself, moving from a predominantly Jewish sect to a largely Gentile, distinct religion that we now call Christianity. This transformation was not planned in advance; it emerged from a series of historical developments: the separation from Judaism, the spread of Gentile churches, the formation of a shared canon and creeds, and the experience of both persecution and, eventually, imperial favor.

By the early 4th century, when Emperor Constantine legalized Christianity and later emperors gave it privileged status, the movement had a recognizable institutional shape: bishops overseeing regional churches, councils articulating common doctrine, and a network of communities linked by shared scripture, worship practices, and a growing sense of belonging to a single ‘catholic’ (universal) church. The story of the origins of Christianity, in this sense, extends beyond the New Testament period into the era when the church’s identity crystallized in ways that would shape its future for centuries.

sections.title: Context
body: The 2nd and 3rd centuries were a time of both growth and contestation for the Christian movement. Christian communities spread into new regions, including North Africa, Gaul, and parts of Asia Minor and Mesopotamia. At the same time, internal diversity increased: different groups proposed different teachings about Jesus, salvation, and the relationship between the Old and New Testaments. Some movements, later labeled ‘gnostic,’ emphasized secret knowledge and sharply contrasted the material and spiritual worlds. Others, such as Marcion’s movement, rejected the Old Testament entirely.

Externally, Christians sometimes faced suspicion and sporadic persecution. They were accused of atheism (for refusing to worship the gods), of immorality (due to misunderstandings of their private meetings and talk of ‘love feasts’), and of disloyalty to the emperor. Persecutions were usually local and limited, but in the mid‑3rd century under Decius and again in the early 4th century under Diocletian, empire‑wide efforts were made to force Christians to sacrifice to the gods or face punishment. These pressures raised questions about how to treat those who lapsed under persecution and then sought to return, debates that shaped later church discipline.

sections.title: Key figures & forces
body: Church fathers such as Ignatius of Antioch, Irenaeus of Lyons, Tertullian of Carthage, and Origen of Alexandria played key roles in shaping emerging Christian identity. Ignatius emphasized the importance of unity under the leadership of bishops and warned against both Judaizing tendencies and docetic views that denied Christ’s real humanity. Irenaeus argued against gnostic teachers by appealing to the ‘rule of faith’ and the fourfold Gospel, presenting Christianity as a public, apostolic tradition rather than a secret knowledge for elites.

Emperor Constantine’s conversion and the Edict of Milan in 313 CE, which granted Christians legal toleration, marked a turning point. Suddenly, a once‑marginal movement had imperial favor. Constantine convened the Council of Nicaea in 325 to address the Arian controversy over Christ’s divinity, leading to the Nicene Creed — a statement of faith that affirmed Jesus as ‘true God from true God.’ Imperial involvement in church affairs accelerated processes of doctrinal definition and institutional consolidation, even as it created new tensions about the relationship between spiritual and political authority.

sections.title: Core idea
body: The transition from sect to religion involved the gradual emergence of a shared Christian ‘identity kit’: scripture, creed, and church structure. Scripture meant the Old Testament read in light of the New. Creed meant brief summaries of faith — like the Apostles’ Creed and the Nicene Creed — used in baptism and teaching to articulate who Jesus is and what Christians believe about God, creation, salvation, and the church. Church structure meant an organized hierarchy of leadership, with bishops seen as successors to the apostles, presbyters (priests) assisting them, and deacons serving practical needs.

This identity kit helped distinguish Christianity from both Judaism and other religious movements in the empire. It provided boundaries: to be Christian was to confess certain beliefs, participate in certain rites (baptism and Eucharist), and belong to a community under recognized leaders. At the same time, significant diversity remained in liturgical practices, local customs, and theological emphases. Unity did not mean uniformity; it meant a shared core within which legitimate variation could exist.

sections.title: Actions / how it works
body: On the ground, Christian identity was formed through repeated practices. Catechumens — those preparing for baptism — underwent a period of instruction in Christian teaching and moral life. Baptism, often celebrated at Easter, marked a decisive entry into the community. Regular gatherings for worship included readings from scripture, preaching, prayers, the exchange of peace, and the celebration of the Eucharist, in which believers shared bread and wine as participation in Christ’s body and blood. Care for the poor, widows, orphans, and the sick was a hallmark of Christian communities and impressed many pagan observers.

Councils and synods brought together bishops from different regions to resolve disputes and articulate common teaching. The decisions of these gatherings, while not always accepted immediately or universally, contributed to a growing sense of belonging to one catholic (universal) church rather than to isolated local groups. Martyr stories, circulating widely, offered models of courageous witness and reinforced the idea that to follow Christ might mean suffering and death. All these practices together formed Christians’ imaginations and loyalties.

sections.title: Consequences / impact
body: By the time Christianity emerged as a legally recognized and then privileged religion, it had developed a distinct identity that would shape Western and global history. Its scriptures, creeds, and institutions provided a framework that could be transmitted across cultures and centuries. Christian ideas about God, the person of Jesus, human dignity, sin, grace, and hope for resurrection permeated art, law, philosophy, and social norms in societies influenced by the church.

At the same time, the transition from marginal sect to imperial religion brought new risks. Alignment with political power sometimes compromised the radical ethical vision of Jesus and the early church. Coercion in matters of belief, which had been used against Christians, eventually was used by some Christians against others and against Jews and pagans. Understanding Christianity’s origins as a Jewish movement that grew, diversified, and institutionalized within the Roman world can help contemporary readers appreciate both its enduring strengths and its historical failures.

sections.title: Connections
body: This final concept pulls together the threads of the entire topic. The Second Temple Jewish context, the life and death of Jesus, the resurrection faith, the Jerusalem church, Paul’s mission, tensions with Judaism, the Roman environment, and the formation of scripture all feed into the emergence of Christianity as a distinct, trans‑ethnic religion with a recognizable structure and set of beliefs. The story does not end here — later centuries would see further divisions, reforms, and global expansion — but by the 4th century, the basic contours of what we call ‘Christianity’ were in place.
